summary: Atwood explores the fundamental philosophical difference between PC and Mac enthusiasts: PC users value the hands-on process of customization and personal investment, while Mac users appreciate polished, pre-designed products. He draws a parallel between hardware customization (case modding, overclocking) and software customization (open source, utilities, GUI tweaks). The post argues that the DIY ethic pervades the entire PC ecosystem, from hardware builds to Linux development to Windows power-user configurations. Atwood concludes that while he appreciates Apple's elegant integration of hardware and software, he personally derives more satisfaction from the tinkering process itself.
key_points: PC enthusiasts take pride not in ownership but in what they've personally done to make their equipment unique
The DIY ethic extends from hardware customization to open source software development
Open source is analogous to hot-rodding cars - you can tweak and customize, but with uncertain effects on overall quality
Even users of commercial software engage in DIY behavior through utilities, tweaks, and GUI modifications
Apple offers elegance out of the box; PC offers the satisfaction of building something yourself
money_quotes: What such people are proud of is not mere ownership of the equipment, but what they've done to it to make it what it is.
We make the beige box what we want it to be.

Apple's G5 systems are the product of a world-class design team and stamped out by the thousands; custom PC builds are the highly individual result of dozens or even hundreds of hours of personal investment.
Isn't building custom software a little bit like hot-rodding your automobile?
While I certainly appreciate Apple's ability to box up elegant hardware designs with an elegant UNIX GUI makeover, that just isn't for me. I have more fun when I do it myself.
key_insight: The true appeal of the PC platform lies not in the end result but in the hands-on satisfaction of personal customization and investment.
